description.summary: Akaname ('filth-licker') is a small Japanese yokai recorded in Edo-period bestiaries that emerges in dirty, neglected bathrooms and bathhouses to lick the grime off tubs and floors with its long red tongue. It functions in folklore as a moral spur to keep bathrooms clean.
description.full: Akaname (垢嘗, 'filth-licker') is a yokai of Japanese folklore, classically depicted as a small, child-sized, naked humanoid with shaggy hair, clawed feet, and an extremely long red tongue. It appears in old bathhouses and dilapidated bathrooms at night, where it laps up the dirt, sweat-residue, and mildew (collectively aka) accumulated on tubs, floors, and walls. The earliest known reference is the variant akaneburi described in Yamaoka Genrin's Kokon hyakumonogatari hyōban (1686), and the standard form is fixed by Toriyama Sekien's woodblock-print bestiary Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776). Detailed prose anecdotes appear in Kanda Genki's Nittō honzō zusan (1780). In its conventional form akaname is harmless: rather than attacking humans, it serves as a folk warning that bathrooms left dirty will attract supernatural vermin. A more sinister variant in some regional traditions takes the form of a beautiful woman who licks her victims to skeletal remains, but this is far less widespread than the harmless cleanliness-totem version.

etymology.origin: Japanese, from aka (垢, 'dirt, sweat-grime, scum') and the verb nameru (嘗める, 'to lick').
etymology.first_use: 1686 — earliest reference is to akaneburi in Yamaoka Genrin's Kokon hyakumonogatari hyōban; the form akaname is named in Toriyama Sekien's Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776).

related_phenomena.name: Tenjōname
related_phenomena.description: Ceiling-licker yokai of Toriyama Sekien's catalogues

related_phenomena.name: Aka Manto
related_phenomena.description: Modern bathroom-related Japanese urban legend, sometimes confused with the akaname

related_phenomena.name: Bannik
related_phenomena.description: Slavic bathhouse spirit with comparable role

features.description: Roughly the size of a child, often described in classical sources as having a naked, ruddy or red body.
Conspicuously long red tongue, used to lick filth from bath surfaces.
Sharp claws on the toes; in some descriptions only a single toe per foot.
Cropped or pebbly textured head per the Nittō honzō zusan.
Large round eyes, contributing to the goblin-like appearance.

physical.morphology: Small humanoid yokai built around an oversized tongue and claws; depicted in colour woodblock prints rather than measured in physical units.

abilities.description: Cleans grime, sweat-residue, and mildew from bathroom surfaces with its long tongue.
Appears unseen at night in unattended bathrooms and bathhouses.

behavior.diet: Aka — accumulated dead-skin grime, sweat residue, soap scum, and mildew
patterns: Avoids occupied or recently scrubbed spaces
Functions as a folk reminder to maintain cleanliness

range.description: A figure of Japanese yokai art and folklore, distributed wherever Edo-period and modern Japanese bathing culture is observed.
regions: Old urban bathhouses and private bathrooms across Japan

first_recorded.source: Yamaoka Genrin, Kokon hyakumonogatari hyōban (1686), under the variant name akaneburi

peak_activity_periods.description: Compilation by Yamaoka Genrin (1686), Toriyama Sekien (1776), and Kanda Genki (1780)

origins.cultural_roots: Japanese folk belief regarding household and bathhouse spirits, formalised in Edo-period yokai literature and woodblock-print bestiaries.
origins.earliest_accounts: Yamaoka Genrin's Kokon hyakumonogatari hyōban (1686) is the earliest known printed reference, under the variant name akaneburi.
origins.folklore_connections: Belongs to a network of bathroom and household yokai that includes the ceiling-licker tenjōname and the modern urban legend Aka Manto.

timeline.event: Yamaoka Genrin includes the variant akaneburi in Kokon hyakumonogatari hyōban
Toriyama Sekien depicts and names the akaname in Gazu Hyakki Yagyō
timeline.significance: Standard visual representation; fixes the name 'akaname'

timeline.event: Kanda Genki's Nittō honzō zusan provides further descriptive anecdotes
timeline.significance: Adds the 'pebbly head, round eyes, long tongue' description

history.modern_era: The akaname is one of the most widely recognised minor yokai in modern Japanese popular culture and a stock figure in yokai illustration, manga, and games. Modern English-language documentation, especially through Matthew Meyer's yokai.com, has spread the figure internationally.

documentary.name: Gazu Hyakki Yagyō entry
documentary.description: Toriyama Sekien's illustrated entry that fixes the name and standard appearance of the akaname.
documentary.analysis: An original Edo-period printed source; foundation of all later representations.

documentary.name: Kokon hyakumonogatari hyōban entry
documentary.description: Yamaoka Genrin's earliest reference to the akaneburi (filth-licking) variant.

documentary.name: Nittō honzō zusan entry
documentary.description: Kanda Genki's prose description of the akaname with the 'pebbly head, round eyes, long tongue' language.

paranormal.name: Manifestation of accumulated filth
paranormal.description: In Japanese folk understanding, the akaname is generated where dust and grime concentrate in old bathhouses and dilapidated homes — a literal embodiment of accumulated negative energy or impurity.
evidence_cited: Habitat consistently restricted to neglected bathrooms

skeptical.name: Domestic moral fable
skeptical.description: Folklorists generally treat the akaname as a didactic fable encouraging hygiene: parents and elders invoked it to motivate children to keep bathrooms clean.
evidence_cited: The yokai's exclusive habitat in dirty rooms
Its non-violent role in canonical Edo sources

stories: Toriyama Sekien's depiction of the akaname in Gazu Hyakki Yagyō
Regional variant of a beautiful-woman akaname who licks victims to skeletal remains
folklore.beliefs: The akaname is taken as a folk reminder to maintain cleanliness; an unkempt bath is said to invite it.

literature: Matthew Meyer — The Night Parade of One Hundred Demons and other yokai compendia
games: Recurring as a minor enemy in Japanese console RPGs and yokai-themed games
other: Featured prominently on yokai.com and other modern yokai databases

researchers.name: Yamaoka Genrin
researchers.contribution: Earliest printed reference to the akaneburi variant (1686)

researchers.name: Toriyama Sekien
researchers.contribution: Fixed the standard name and image of the akaname in Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776)

researchers.name: Kanda Genki
researchers.contribution: Provided detailed prose description in Nittō honzō zusan (1780)

researchers.name: Matthew Meyer
researchers.contribution: English-language documentation and translation of the akaname tradition

research.ongoing_studies: Continued scholarly translation of Edo-period yokai catalogues and the role of yokai in Japanese folk hygiene and household discourse.

sources.title: Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (画図百鬼夜行)
Kokon hyakumonogatari hyōban (古今百物語評判)
Nittō honzō zusan (日東本草図纂)
